British Tribal-class destroyer

**HMS *Mashona*** was one of 16 [Tribal-class](/wiki/Tribal-class_destroyer_(1936) "Tribal-class destroyer (1936)") [destroyers](/wiki/Destroyer "Destroyer") built for the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") shortly before the beginning of [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") in 1939. Completed in that year, she was assigned to the [Home Fleet](/wiki/Home_Fleet "Home Fleet"). During the first year of the war, the ship was on [convoy](/wiki/Convoy "Convoy") escort duties. *Mashona* played an active role in the [Norwegian Campaign](/wiki/Norwegian_Campaign "Norwegian Campaign") of April–May 1940, escorting convoys to and from Norway. The ship helped to briefly seize four [Swedish](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sweden "Kingdom of Sweden") warships en route from Italy to Sweden in June 1940 in what became known as the [Psilander affair](/wiki/Psilander_affair "Psilander affair") and resumed her role of convoy escort after their release in July. She accidentally collided with one of her [sister ships](/wiki/Sister_ship "Sister ship") in early 1941 and spent several months under repair.

*Mashona* was escorting the [battleship](/wiki/Battleship "Battleship") [HMS *Rodney*](/wiki/HMS_Rodney_(29) "HMS Rodney (29)") in May when they were diverted to [search](/wiki/Last_battle_of_the_battleship_Bismarck "Last battle of the battleship Bismarck") for the [German battleship *Bismarck*](/wiki/German_battleship_Bismarck "German battleship Bismarck"). The destroyer was forced to abandon the search for a lack of fuel shortly before the German ship was sunk on 27 May 1941. As *Mashona* was headed home, she was attacked and sunk by [Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe") [bombers](/wiki/Bomber "Bomber") the following day, although nearby ships were able to rescue 184 of her crew.

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HMS_Mashona&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description")]

The Tribal-class destroyers were intended to counter the large destroyers being built abroad and to improve the firepower of the existing destroyer [flotillas](/wiki/Flotilla "Flotilla") and were thus significantly larger and more heavily armed than the preceding [I class](/wiki/I-class_destroyer "I-class destroyer").[[2]](#cite_note-2) The ships displaced 1,891 [long tons](/wiki/Long_ton "Long ton") (1,921 [t](/wiki/Tonne "Tonne")) at [standard](/wiki/Displacement_(ship) "Displacement (ship)") load and 2,519 long tons (2,559 t) at [deep load](/wiki/Deep_load "Deep load").[[3]](#cite_note-3) They had an [overall length](/wiki/Length_overall "Length overall") of 377 feet (114.9 m), a [beam](/wiki/Beam_(nautical) "Beam (nautical)") of 36 feet 6 inches (11.13 m)[[4]](#cite_note-l65-4) and a [draught](/wiki/Draft_(hull) "Draft (hull)") of 11 feet 3 inches (3.43 m).[[5]](#cite_note-e12-5) The destroyers were powered by two [Parsons](/wiki/Parsons_Marine_Steam_Turbine_Company "Parsons Marine Steam Turbine Company") geared [steam turbines](/wiki/Steam_turbine "Steam turbine"), each driving one [propeller shaft](/wiki/Propeller_shaft "Propeller shaft") using steam provided by three [Admiralty three-drum boilers](/wiki/Admiralty_three-drum_boiler "Admiralty three-drum boiler"). The turbines developed a total of 44,000 [shaft horsepower](/wiki/Horsepower#Shaft_horsepower "Horsepower") (33,000 [kW](/wiki/Kilowatt "Kilowatt")) and gave a maximum speed of 36 [knots](/wiki/Knot_(unit) "Knot (unit)") (67 km/h; 41 mph).[[4]](#cite_note-l65-4) During her [sea trials](/wiki/Sea_trial "Sea trial") *Mashona* made 35.3 knots (65.4 km/h; 40.6 mph) from 45,437 shp (33,882 kW) at a displacement of 1,990 long tons (2,020 t).[[6]](#cite_note-6) The ships carried enough [fuel oil](/wiki/Fuel_oil "Fuel oil") to give them a range of 5,700 [nautical miles](/wiki/Nautical_mile "Nautical mile") (10,600 km; 6,600 mi) at 15 knots (28 km/h; 17 mph).[[5]](#cite_note-e12-5) The ships' complement consisted of 190 officers and [ratings](/wiki/Naval_rating "Naval rating"), although the [flotilla leaders](/wiki/Flotilla_leader "Flotilla leader") carried an extra 20 officers and men consisting of the [Captain (D)](/wiki/Captain_(D) "Captain (D)") and his staff.[[7]](#cite_note-w99-7)

The primary armament of the Tribal-class was eight [quick-firing (QF)](/wiki/List_of_British_ordnance_terms#QF "List of British ordnance terms") [4.7-inch (120 mm) Mark XII guns](/wiki/4.7_inch_QF_Mark_XII "4.7 inch QF Mark XII") in four twin-gun mounts, one pair each fore and aft of the [superstructure](/wiki/Superstructure "Superstructure"), designated 'A', 'B', 'X', and 'Y' from front to rear. The mounts had a maximum [elevation](/wiki/Elevation_(ballistics) "Elevation (ballistics)") of 40°. For [anti-aircraft (AA)](/wiki/Anti-aircraft_warfare "Anti-aircraft warfare") defence, they carried a single quadruple mount for the 40-millimetre (1.6 in) [QF two-pounder Mk II](/wiki/QF_2_pounder_naval_gun "QF 2 pounder naval gun") "pom-pom" gun and two quadruple mounts for the [0.5-inch (12.7 mm) Mark III](/wiki/Vickers_.50_machine_gun "Vickers .50 machine gun") [machine gun](/wiki/Machine_gun "Machine gun").[[8]](#cite_note-8) Low-angle fire for the main guns was controlled by the [director-control tower (DCT)](/wiki/Director_(military) "Director (military)") on the [bridge](/wiki/Bridge_(nautical) "Bridge (nautical)") roof that fed data acquired by it and the 12-foot (3.7 m) [rangefinder](/wiki/Rangefinding_telemeter "Rangefinding telemeter") on the Mk II Rangefinder/Director directly aft of the DCT to an analogue [mechanical computer](/wiki/Mechanical_computer "Mechanical computer"), the Mk I [Admiralty Fire Control Clock](/wiki/Admiralty_Fire_Control_Clock "Admiralty Fire Control Clock"). Anti-aircraft fire for the main guns was controlled by the Rangefinder/Director which sent data to the mechanical [Fuze Keeping Clock](/wiki/Fuze_Keeping_Clock "Fuze Keeping Clock").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The ships were fitted with a single above-water quadruple mount for [21-inch (533 mm)](/wiki/British_21_inch_torpedo "British 21 inch torpedo") torpedoes.[[7]](#cite_note-w99-7) The Tribals were not intended as anti-submarine ships, but they were provided with [ASDIC](/wiki/ASDIC "ASDIC"), one [depth charge](/wiki/Depth_charge "Depth charge") rack and two throwers for self-defence, although the throwers were not mounted in all ships;[[10]](#cite_note-10) Twenty depth charges was the peacetime allotment, but this increased to 30 during wartime.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Wartime modifications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HMS_Mashona&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Wartime modifications")]

Heavy losses to German air attack during the [Norwegian Campaign](/wiki/Norwegian_Campaign "Norwegian Campaign") demonstrated the ineffectiveness of the Tribals' anti-aircraft suite and the RN decided in May 1940 to replace 'X' mount with a twin-gun mount for [QF 4-inch (102 mm) Mark XVI](/wiki/QF_4_inch_Mk_XVI_naval_gun "QF 4 inch Mk XVI naval gun") [dual-purpose guns](/wiki/Dual-purpose_gun "Dual-purpose gun"); *Mashona* had hers fitted during her August–October 1940 refit. The ship may have been fitted with a [Type 285](/wiki/Type_285_radar "Type 285 radar") [gunnery radar](/wiki/Fire-control_radar "Fire-control radar") on the roof of the rangefinder/director.[[12]](#cite_note-12) To increase the firing arcs of the AA guns, the rear [funnel](/wiki/Funnel_(ship) "Funnel (ship)") was shortened and the [mainmast](/wiki/Mainmast "Mainmast") was reduced to a short pole mast.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The number of depth charges was increased to 46 early in the war.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Construction and career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HMS_Mashona&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Construction and career")]

Authorized as one of nine Tribal-class destroyers under the 1936 Naval Estimates,[[15]](#cite_note-15) *Mashona* has been the only ship of her name to serve in the Royal Navy.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The ship was ordered on 19 June 1936 from Parsons, but her hull was subcontracted to [Vickers-Armstrong](/wiki/Vickers-Armstrong "Vickers-Armstrong") and was [laid down](/wiki/Laid_down "Laid down") on 5 August at the company's High [Walker, Newcastle upon Tyne](/wiki/Walker,_Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Walker, Newcastle upon Tyne"), [shipyard](/wiki/Shipyard "Shipyard"). [Launched](/wiki/Ceremonial_ship_launching "Ceremonial ship launching") on 3 September 1937, *Mashona* was completed on 30 March 1939 and [commissioned](/wiki/Ship_commissioning "Ship commissioning") on 1 April at a cost of [£](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling")341,108 which excluded weapons and communications outfits furnished by the [Admiralty](/wiki/British_Admiralty "British Admiralty"). Her construction had been delayed by the late delivery of her gun mountings. Initially assigned to the [Sixth Destroyer Flotilla](/wiki/Sixth_Destroyer_Flotilla "Sixth Destroyer Flotilla") (DF) of the Home Fleet, under the command of [Commander](/wiki/Commander_(Royal_Navy) "Commander (Royal Navy)") [Patrick McLaughlin](/w/index.php?title=Patrick_McLaughlin_(Royal_Navy_officer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Patrick McLaughlin (Royal Navy officer) (page does not exist)"), the ship escorted the [ocean liner](/wiki/Ocean_liner "Ocean liner") [*Empress of Australia*](/wiki/RMS_Empress_of_Australia_(1919) "RMS Empress of Australia (1919)") through the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel") in May with King [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI") and [Queen Elizabeth](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_The_Queen_Mother "Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother") aboard as they departed for [their tour of Canada](/wiki/1939_royal_tour_of_Canada "1939 royal tour of Canada"). The following month she helped to search for the sunken [submarine](/wiki/Submarine "Submarine") [*Thetis*](/wiki/HMS_Thetis_(N25) "HMS Thetis (N25)") after a diving accident.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

When Great Britain declared war on Nazi Germany on 3 September 1939, *Mashona* and the bulk of the Home Fleet were unsuccessfully patrolling the waters between [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland"), [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") and [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") for German [blockade runners](/wiki/Blockade_runner "Blockade runner"). The [2nd Cruiser Squadron](/wiki/2nd_Cruiser_Squadron "2nd Cruiser Squadron"), including *Mashona*, was already at sea when the submarine [*Spearfish*](/wiki/HMS_Spearfish "HMS Spearfish"), on patrol in the [Heligoland Bight](/wiki/Heligoland_Bight "Heligoland Bight"), was badly damaged by German depth charges on 24 September. Unable to submerge, she requested assistance and the squadron responded with two [destroyers](/wiki/Destroyers "Destroyers") escorting her home. The rest of the squadron, backed by the bulk of the Home Fleet, provided [cover](/wiki/Covering_force "Covering force"). The Germans spotted the squadron and unsuccessfully attacked the ships with nine bombers from the first [group](/wiki/Group_(military_unit) "Group (military unit)") of Bomber [Wing](/wiki/Wing_(air_force_unit) "Wing (air force unit)") 26 (I/[Kampfgeschwader 26](/wiki/Kampfgeschwader_26 "Kampfgeschwader 26")). *Mashona* was present in [Scapa Flow](/wiki/Scapa_Flow "Scapa Flow") when the [German submarine *U-47*](/wiki/German_submarine_U-47_(1938) "German submarine U-47 (1938)") sank the [battleship](/wiki/Battleship "Battleship") [*Royal Oak*](/wiki/HMS_Royal_Oak_(08) "HMS Royal Oak (08)") on the night of 13/14 October. By the time the British realised that the battleship had been torpedoed and *Mashona* became the first ship to search for the submarine, some two hours had passed and *U-47* had reached the open sea.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The destroyer was refitted from 25 October to 10 November 1939 in [South Shields](/wiki/South_Shields "South Shields"). Following the sinking of the [armed merchant cruiser](/wiki/Armed_merchant_cruiser "Armed merchant cruiser") [*Rawalpindi*](/wiki/HMS_Rawalpindi "HMS Rawalpindi") on 23 November by the German battleships [*Scharnhorst*](/wiki/German_battleship_Scharnhorst "German battleship Scharnhorst") and [*Gneisenau*](/wiki/German_battleship_Gneisenau "German battleship Gneisenau") off Iceland, *Mashona* and the rest of the Home Fleet hunted the enemy ships, but heavy weather allowed them to evade their pursuers and return to Germany. The ship spent the next several months on convoy escort and patrol duties and Commander [William Selby](/w/index.php?title=William_Selby_(Royal_Navy_officer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "William Selby (Royal Navy officer) (page does not exist)") relieved McLaughlin on 23 January 1940. Around that time, she underwent a refit at [Chatham Naval Dockyard](/wiki/Chatham_Naval_Dockyard "Chatham Naval Dockyard") that ended in March.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Norwegian Campaign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HMS_Mashona&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Norwegian Campaign")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/1940NorwayCampaign.jpg/500px-1940NorwayCampaign.jpg)](/wiki/File:1940NorwayCampaign.jpg)

Maps covering the operations in central and southern Norway, April–May 1940

Receiving word that the [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force") had attacked north-bound German warships in the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") on 7 April 1940, the Home Fleet put to sea that evening. The 2nd Cruiser Squadron departed [Rosyth](/wiki/Rosyth "Rosyth"), Scotland, with its two [light cruisers](/wiki/Light_cruiser "Light cruiser") with orders to sweep through the North Sea before rendezvousing with the main body of the Home Fleet. The 6th DF with *Mashona*, her sisters [*Somali*](/wiki/HMS_Somali "HMS Somali"), [*Matabele*](/wiki/HMS_Matabele "HMS Matabele"), and [*Tartar*](/wiki/HMS_Tartar_(F43) "HMS Tartar (F43)"), rendezvoused with the squadron the following morning. On the morning of 9 April the [4th Destroyer Flotilla](/wiki/4th_Destroyer_Flotilla "4th Destroyer Flotilla"), now including *Mashona*, was tasked on attacking [Bergen](/wiki/Bergen "Bergen"), Norway, covered by the [18th Cruiser Squadron](/wiki/18th_Cruiser_Squadron "18th Cruiser Squadron"), but the Admiralty cancelled the attack that afternoon when it received reports that two German light cruisers were in port. As the British ships were withdrawing, they were attacked by 88 bombers of Bomber Wing 26 and Bomber Wing 30 ([Kampfgeschwader 30](/wiki/Kampfgeschwader_30 "Kampfgeschwader 30")), sinking *Mashona*'s sister [*Gurkha*](/wiki/HMS_Gurkha_(F20) "HMS Gurkha (F20)") and lightly damaged the battleship [*Rodney*](/wiki/HMS_Rodney_(29) "HMS Rodney (29)").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

After refuelling at Scapa Flow the following day, *Mashona*, five of her sisters and two light cruisers departed on the evening of 11 April, arriving off [Stadlandet](/wiki/Stadlandet "Stadlandet") the following morning. The destroyers were split up to search the area for German ships before rendezvousing with the cruisers at dusk, but an inaccurate spot report of a German [battlecruiser](/wiki/Battlecruiser "Battlecruiser") and cruiser that afternoon, forestalled the searches as the destroyers were recalled. On the morning of 13 April the destroyers were sent to search the [Romsdalsfjord](/wiki/Romsdalsfjord "Romsdalsfjord") and only found four merchant ships. As they were leaving [Ålesund](/wiki/%C3%85lesund "Ålesund") they were unsuccessfully attacked by a dozen bombers from III Group, Demonstration Wing 1 ([Lehrgeschwader 1](/wiki/Lehrgeschwader_1 "Lehrgeschwader 1")). The following morning they were ordered north to the [Namsos](/wiki/Namsos_(town) "Namsos (town)") area to examine its suitability for an Allied landing and to coordinate with local Norwegian forces. Harbour facilities were assessed as inadequate and that troops should be landed elsewhere and transferred to destroyers for off-loading at Namsos. The Admiralty ordered that the [148th Infantry Brigade](/wiki/148th_Infantry_Brigade_(United_Kingdom) "148th Infantry Brigade (United Kingdom)"), already at sea, to be diverted to the anchorage at Lillesjona; its [troopships](/wiki/Troopship "Troopship") arrived there at dawn on 16 April and began transferring their troops to the destroyers after they had completed refuelling. Half-a-dozen Luftwaffe bombers disrupted the transfer that afternoon with little effect. The destroyers unloaded their troops that night and the rest of the troops arrived the following evening. The destroyers and their covering cruisers were ordered home on 19 April, *Mashona* and [*Sikh*](/wiki/HMS_Sikh_(F82) "HMS Sikh (F82)") escorting the troopship [MS *Chrobry*](/wiki/MS_Chrobry "MS Chrobry").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The Allied defeat during the Namsos Campaign forced them to evacuate the survivors. *Mashona* picked up the troops at [Veblungsnes](/wiki/Veblungsnes "Veblungsnes") on the evening of 30 April and then helped to ferry troops from the small [wharf](/wiki/Wharf "Wharf") at [Åndalsnes](/wiki/%C3%85ndalsnes "Åndalsnes") out to the cruisers anchored in the harbour the following night. On 6 May, the destroyer was one of the escorts for the [aircraft carrier](/wiki/Aircraft_carrier "Aircraft carrier") [*Ark Royal*](/wiki/HMS_Ark_Royal_(91) "HMS Ark Royal (91)") in the [Narvik](/wiki/Narvik "Narvik") area. The following month *Mashona* helped to escort the battleship [*Valiant*](/wiki/HMS_Valiant_(1914) "HMS Valiant (1914)") as she covered the evacuation from Narvik on in early June. The destroyer searched without success for any survivors from the aircraft carrier [*Glorious*](/wiki/HMS_Glorious "HMS Glorious") after she had been sunk by *Gneisenau* and *Scharnhorst* on 8 June.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Subsequent operations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HMS_Mashona&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Subsequent operations")]

*Mashona*, *Tartar* and their sister [*Maori*](/wiki/HMS_Maori_(F24) "HMS Maori (F24)") were ordered on 19 June 1940 to seize four Swedish destroyers that had been purchased from [Fascist Italy](/wiki/Fascist_Italy_(1922%E2%80%931943) "Fascist Italy (1922–1943)") and were on their delivery voyage, citing the right of [angary](/wiki/Angary "Angary"), which allows for the taking of neutral property to prevent its use by the enemy. The Swedish government had informed the British and the Germans of this purchase and of the ships' route, so the British destroyers were able to blockade the Swedish ships in [Skálafjørður](/wiki/Sk%C3%A1lafj%C3%B8r%C3%B0ur "Skálafjørður") in the [Faeroe Islands](/wiki/Faeroe_Islands "Faeroe Islands") the following morning, despite their earlier guarantee of freedom of passage. The Swedish crews were transferred under protest to their accompanying ocean liner, [SS *Patricia*](/w/index.php?title=SS_Patricia&action=edit&redlink=1 "SS Patricia (page does not exist)"), and [oil tanker](/wiki/Oil_tanker "Oil tanker"), [SS *Castor*](/w/index.php?title=SS_Castor&action=edit&redlink=1 "SS Castor (page does not exist)"), and the British ships put crews aboard two of the destroyers, [*Puke*](/wiki/HSwMS_Puke "HSwMS Puke") and [*Remus*](/wiki/HSwMS_Remus "HSwMS Remus"). The two Swedish destroyers sailed for Scapa Flow, escorted by *Tartar*, on 21 June, while the two Swedish civilian ships headed for Sweden. The commander of the Swedish force informed his government about the seizure and was ordered to return his two ships to [Tórshavn](/wiki/T%C3%B3rshavn "Tórshavn") and wait there until the ships were returned; *Mashona* also sailed to Tórshavn to prevent the Swedes from retaking possession of their ships. During the voyage to Scapa, *Puke* broke down on 22 June and had to be towed; she did not reach her destination for another week. In the meantime, *Mashona* escorted the other two other destroyers to Scapa where they arrived on the 26th. The ocean liner arrived on 30 June and the British returned the ships to the Swedes on 2 July in response to the strong Swedish protests.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

While escorting the Home Fleet on 27 June in the North Sea, *Mashona* was damaged by a [freak wave](/wiki/Freak_wave "Freak wave") that dropped her into a deep trough; the impact damaged her bottom plating and sprung 240 [rivets](/wiki/Rivet "Rivet"). The ship had to return to Rosyth for repairs that included strengthening the bottom of her hull and resumed her escort duties afterwards. She began a refit at [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool") in August and was inspected by King George VI and Queen Elizabeth there before work began. Upon its completion on 5 October, *Mashona* was assigned escort duties in the [Western Approaches](/wiki/Western_Approaches "Western Approaches") and rescued survivors from the torpedoed [cargo liner](/wiki/Cargo_liner "Cargo liner") [SS *Rotorua*](/wiki/SS_Rotorua "SS Rotorua") on 11 December. While departing Scapa at night on 6 January 1941, *Mashona* accidentally rammed *Sikh* and was under repair in [West Hartlepool](/wiki/West_Hartlepool "West Hartlepool") from 16 January to 3 March. *Mashona* spent the next several months escorting units of the Home Fleet.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Map_Rheinuebung.svg/250px-Map_Rheinuebung.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_Rheinuebung.svg)

Map of [Operation Rheinübung](/wiki/Operation_Rhein%C3%BCbung "Operation Rheinübung") and Royal Navy operations against the *Bismarck*

On 22 May 1941 the ship formed part of the escort for *Rodney* and the ocean liner [MV *Britannic*](/wiki/MV_Britannic "MV Britannic") as they set sail for [Halifax](/wiki/Halifax_Regional_Municipality "Halifax Regional Municipality"), Canada. After the [German battleship *Bismarck*](/wiki/German_battleship_Bismarck "German battleship Bismarck") sank the battlecruiser [*Hood*](/wiki/HMS_Hood "HMS Hood") during the [Battle of Denmark Strait](/wiki/Battle_of_Denmark_Strait "Battle of Denmark Strait") two days later, *Rodney* was ordered by the Admiralty to join in the pursuit of the German ship, taking *Mashona*, *Tartar* and *Somali* with her in the search. By 26 May, the *Bismarck* had been located by a [maritime patrol aircraft](/wiki/Maritime_patrol_aircraft "Maritime patrol aircraft") and *Mashona* was sent to find [Admiral](/wiki/Admiral "Admiral") Sir [John Tovey](/wiki/John_Tovey,_1st_Baron_Tovey "John Tovey, 1st Baron Tovey") in his flagship, the battleship [*King George V*](/wiki/HMS_King_George_V_(41) "HMS King George V (41)"). The destroyer was tasked to relay *Rodney*'s location, using a [signal lamp](/wiki/Signal_lamp "Signal lamp") to avoid alerting the Germans of the presence of any nearby British ships via [wireless intercepts](/wiki/Signals_intelligence "Signals intelligence"). The battleships rendezvoused later that afternoon and Tovey was informed of *Rodney*'s fuel shortage as well as that of her escorts. That evening *Mashona*, *Tartar* and *Somali* were forced to turn back for lack of fuel.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

On the morning of 28 May 1941, *Mashona* and *Tartar* were attacked by numerous Luftwaffe bombers while returning to port at a speed of 15 knots (28 km/h; 17 mph). A [Heinkel He 111](/wiki/Heinkel_He_111 "Heinkel He 111") of the first group of Bomber Wing 28 (I/[Kampfgeschwader 28](/w/index.php?title=Kampfgeschwader_28&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kampfgeschwader 28 (page does not exist)")) hit *Mashona* with a single bomb in the forward [boiler room](/wiki/Fire_room "Fire room") that caused severe flooding and killed 46 men. Dead in the water with a growing [list](/wiki/List_(watercraft) "List (watercraft)"), the flooding could not be contained and the crew was forced to abandon ship before she [capsized](/wiki/Capsized "Capsized"). During a lull in the bombing, *Tartar* was able to rescue 184 survivors, although 10 subsequently died of [hypothermia](/wiki/Hypothermia "Hypothermia"), but missed with a torpedo when she tried to [scuttle](/wiki/Scuttling "Scuttling") her sister. The floating [hulk](/wiki/Hulk_(ship_type) "Hulk (ship type)") was finally sunk off the coast of [Galway](/wiki/Galway "Galway"), Ireland, at [52°58′N 11°36′W﻿ / ﻿52.967°N 11.600°W﻿ / 52.967; -11.600](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=HMS_Mashona&params=52_58_N_11_36_W_) by shells from a pair of Canadian destroyers that arrived that afternoon.[[26]](#cite_note-26)
